description:
You are the backup administrator for this workstation.  
A small data set that must be archived already exists at  

    /home/user/data_to_backup  

The goal is to produce a minimal, **verifiable** backup bundle and its companion
documentation files in a new directory called  

    /home/user/backups  

The entire job can be accomplished with roughly three or four typical
shell commands (mkdir, tar, sort, sha256sum, etc.).

When you are finished, the directory tree **/home/user/backups** must contain
all three items listed below, created exactly as described.

1. /home/user/backups/data_backup.tar.gz  
   • A gzip-compressed tar archive that contains every file and sub-directory
     found under /home/user/data_to_backup.  
   • The paths inside the archive must be **relative**; the root of the archive
     should correspond to /home/user/data_to_backup itself (not “/” and not
     “home/user/data_to_backup”).  
   • File ordering inside the archive does not matter.

2. /home/user/backups/data_backup_manifest.log  
   • A plain-text file.  
   • One archived path per line, **sorted in pure ASCII/lexicographic order**.  
   • Each line must contain only the relative path (no leading “./”).  
   • The manifest is considered correct if (and only if) its contents are
     byte-for-byte identical to this example format:  
       doc1.txt  
       images/photo.jpg  
       scripts/backup.sh

3. /home/user/backups/data_backup_checksums.log  
   • A plain-text file produced by running the standard “sha256sum” utility
     against every regular file in /home/user/data_to_backup **before** it is
     tarred.  
   • The output format must be the default produced by “sha256sum”, i.e.  
       <64-hex-digit hash><two spaces><relative path>  
     Example (hashes will differ):  
       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4...  doc1.txt  
   • Paths in the checksum file must match the paths used in the manifest and
     must appear in exactly the same order as in data_backup_manifest.log.

Important verification notes (read carefully):

• The test harness will untar data_backup.tar.gz into a temporary directory and
  compare the extracted paths to data_backup_manifest.log — the file must match
  *exactly* (including the final newline character).  
• The test harness will run “sha256sum -c data_backup_checksums.log” from inside
  the temporary extraction directory.  It therefore expects the checksum file
  to contain only the relative paths listed in the manifest.  
• Do **not** add any extra commentary, timestamps, or blank lines to either log
  file.  
• You do not need super-user privileges at any step; everything must be created
  inside your own home directory.

If the three files described above are present and formatted exactly as
specified, the job is complete and the automated verifier will mark the task
as successful.
truth:
Initial data supplied by the task runner (already present before the agent
starts):

Directory tree: /home/user/data_to_backup
├── doc1.txt
│   └── contents: literal text “First document\n”
├── images
│   └── photo.jpg        (non-empty binary file; exact bytes not relevant)
└── scripts
     └── backup.sh       (text file containing “#!/bin/bash\necho backup\n”)

Ground-truth that the verifier will use:

1. The manifest file must be located at
      /home/user/backups/data_backup_manifest.log
   and must contain exactly three lines (each ending in LF):
      doc1.txt\n
      images/photo.jpg\n
      scripts/backup.sh\n

2. The checksum file must be located at
      /home/user/backups/data_backup_checksums.log
   and must contain exactly three lines whose second field (the path) is, in
   order:
      doc1.txt
      images/photo.jpg
      scripts/backup.sh
   The verifier will recalculate the SHA-256 digests of those three files and
   compare them to the hashes found at the beginning of each corresponding line
   (it ignores letter-case distinctions in the hex string).

3. The archive file must be located at
      /home/user/backups/data_backup.tar.gz
   and, when extracted, must yield the three files listed above with paths
   relative to the archive root:
      ./doc1.txt
      ./images/photo.jpg
      ./scripts/backup.sh
name: task_000000_09209ccc
